section.title: The Song of Moses
section.sectionRationale: Israel's first act on the far side of the sea is worship in song. This poem, among the oldest in Scripture, turns the crossing into theology: it names who God is, what He has done, and where He is taking His people, becoming the pattern for how the redeemed remember their redemption.
section.thesis: Freshly delivered from Egypt, Israel sings that the LORD is a warrior who has triumphed gloriously, unmatched among the gods, whose loving devotion will lead His redeemed people to His own holy dwelling, and Miriam leads the women in the same refrain.
section.frameNote: Five movements: the opening praise of the LORD as strength and salvation; the destruction of Pharaoh's army retold as poetry; the incomparable holiness of God; the promise that the nations will tremble while God brings His people to His mountain; and Miriam's answering song.

units.label: The LORD is my strength and my song
units.note: praise for the horse and rider thrown into the sea

verses.text:
Then Moses and the Israelites sang this song to the LORD: 
“I will sing to the LORD, for He is {{exalted|highly exalted}}. The horse and rider He has thrown into the sea.
The LORD is my strength and my song, and He has become my salvation. He is my God, and I will praise Him, my father's God, and I will exalt Him.
The LORD is a {{warrior|warrior}}, the LORD is His name.

units.label: The enemy destroyed
units.note: sank like a stone, like lead

verses.text: Pharaoh's chariots and army He has cast into the sea; the finest of his officers are drowned in the Red Sea.
The depths have covered them; they sank there like a stone.
Your right hand, O LORD, is majestic in power; Your right hand, O LORD, has shattered the enemy.
You overthrew Your adversaries by Your great majesty. You unleashed Your burning wrath; it consumed them like stubble.
At the blast of Your nostrils the waters piled up; like a wall the currents stood firm; the depths congealed in the heart of the sea.
The enemy declared, ‘I will pursue, I will overtake. I will divide the spoils; I will gorge myself on them. I will draw my sword; my hand will destroy them.’
But You blew with Your breath, and the sea covered them. They sank like lead in the mighty waters.

units.label: Who is like You, O LORD

verses.text: Who among the gods is like You, O LORD? Who is like You, majestic in {{holiness|holiness}}, revered with praises, performing wonders?
You stretched out Your right hand, and the earth swallowed them up.
With loving devotion You will lead the people You have redeemed; with Your strength You will guide them to Your holy dwelling.

units.label: The nations tremble, the sanctuary promised

verses.text: The nations will hear and tremble; anguish will grip the dwellers of Philistia.
Then the chiefs of Edom will be dismayed; trembling will seize the leaders of Moab; those who dwell in Canaan will melt away,
and terror and dread will fall on them. By the power of Your arm they will be as still as a stone until Your people pass by, O LORD, until the people You have bought pass by.
You will bring them in and plant them on the mountain of Your inheritance, the place, O LORD, You have prepared for Your dwelling, the sanctuary, O Lord, Your hands have established.
The LORD will reign forever and ever!”
For when Pharaoh's horses, chariots, and horsemen went into the sea, the LORD brought the waters of the sea back over them. But the Israelites walked through the sea on dry ground.
Then Miriam the prophetess, Aaron's sister, took a tambourine in her hand, and all the women followed her with tambourines and dancing.

And Miriam sang back to them: 
“Sing to the LORD, for He is highly exalted; the horse and rider He has thrown into the sea.”

exalted.gloss: To be highly exalted, to triumph gloriously, to rise up in majesty. The very first word of Israel's worship after the crossing is a declaration that God alone has done this, He has triumphed, not Israel.

warrior.gloss: A man of war, a warrior. Israel does not sing that God helped them fight; He alone fought while they stood still, and this song names Him plainly as the warrior whose name is the LORD.

holiness.gloss: Holiness, set-apartness, the very otherness of God. The song's central question, who is like You, majestic in holiness, has no rival answer; God's holiness is what makes Him utterly incomparable among every so-called god of the nations.

basic.context: The moment Israel is safely across the sea, they burst into song, one of the oldest poems in the Bible. It retells what just happened, God alone destroying Pharaoh's army, and looks ahead to God bringing His redeemed people all the way to His holy mountain.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a real song sung by real people at a specific moment of deliverance; it is poetry, using vivid images like the earth swallowing the enemy, but it describes an actual historical rescue. Its purpose is worship and remembrance, teaching every later reader how to respond rightly to God's saving power.
basic.originalLanguages: The song opens declaring God ga'ah, highly exalted, triumphant, crediting Him alone with the victory. It calls Him ish milchamah, a warrior, plainly and without embarrassment. And its central, unanswerable question asks who is like the LORD in qodesh, holiness, His utter set-apartness from every false god.
basic.typology: The song's hope, that God will bring His redeemed people to His own dwelling place, anticipates the whole biblical trajectory toward a people finally and fully at home with God, fulfilled ultimately in the new creation where God dwells with His people forever, Revelation 21:3.
basic.christ: Revelation 15:3 pictures the redeemed in heaven singing the song of Moses and the song of the Lamb together, joining this ancient victory song to the greater and final victory won by Christ, the true and better warrior who triumphs not with a sword but through the cross.
basic.god: God is revealed here as both warrior and shepherd in the same breath, utterly holy and yet leading His people with loving devotion toward His own home. His incomparability is not an abstract claim but is proven by an act every Israelite there had just witnessed with their own eyes.
basic.crossReferences: Revelation 15:3-4 has the redeemed sing the song of Moses and of the Lamb together. Psalm 77:11-20 recalls this same crossing to strengthen faith in a later generation. Isaiah 12 echoes this song's language of the LORD as strength, song, and salvation.
basic.oneStory: Israel's first response to salvation is not relief alone but worship in song, retelling what God has done and trusting Him for what is still ahead. This pattern, redemption answered with praise that looks forward to God's dwelling with His people, runs to Revelation, where the redeemed sing this very song beside the song of the Lamb.

context.literary: Widely regarded by scholars as among the oldest poetry in the Hebrew Bible, the song moves from immediate praise for the crossing just witnessed to a prophetic anticipation of the conquest and settlement still to come, closing with Miriam's antiphonal refrain sung by the women.
context.historical: This song functioned for Israel much like a national anthem of salvation, retelling the founding act of deliverance and rehearsing it for later generations as proof that the LORD who saved them once could be trusted to finish what He began.
context.canonical: As the Bible's first extended song of praise, it establishes a pattern followed throughout the Psalms and prophets, and it is picked up explicitly in Revelation, where it becomes part of the church's final, victorious worship.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To lead God's newly redeemed people in worship that credits the LORD alone for their deliverance, and to root their ongoing hope, for the journey ahead and the land promised, in the character of the God who has just proven Himself their warrior and redeemer.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This is a specific song of praise composed for a particular historical deliverance; it is not a legal text prescribing behavior. Its abiding value lies in its pattern for worship, crediting God fully for salvation, and in the truths it declares about His holiness, power, and covenant faithfulness, truths that remain binding even though the song itself was composed for one moment in Israel's history.

debates.question: How should the song's violent, celebratory language about the drowned Egyptian army be read?

views.label: Righteous joy in God's justice
views.case: The song celebrates God's just judgment on a genocidal oppressor who had ordered Israelite infants killed, Exodus 1:22; joy at the defeat of great cruelty is not the same as bloodthirsty vengeance.

views.label: A call for careful, Christ-shaped reading
views.case: Some readers caution that Christians should read such texts through the fuller revelation of God's character in Christ, who wept even over Jerusalem's coming judgment, holding together genuine praise for justice with sorrow over any person's destruction.

debates.bearing: Both approaches affirm the song's core claim, that God rightly judges oppression and rightly saves His people, while inviting care in how that truth is applied and expressed today.

study.originalLanguages: The song's very first claim is that God ga'ah, is highly exalted, has triumphed, language that refuses to let Israel take any credit for their own rescue. It names Him plainly ish milchamah, a warrior, and its unanswered question, who is like You in qodesh, holiness, sets God utterly apart from every rival power Egypt claimed to serve.

typology.type: The LORD bringing His redeemed people to His own holy dwelling, verse 13
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in stages, the tabernacle, the temple, and finally in the new creation, where God dwells with His people forever, Revelation 21:3.

typology.type: The song of Moses celebrating decisive victory
typology.fulfillment: Joined in Revelation 15:3-4 with the song of the Lamb, sung by the redeemed after the final victory over evil is complete.

study.christ: Revelation 15:3-4 places this very song, the song of Moses, alongside the song of the Lamb in the mouths of the redeemed in heaven, linking Israel's ancient deliverance to the final, complete victory won by Christ. The song's confession, the LORD is my strength and my song, and He has become my salvation, becomes in the New Testament a confession about Jesus, whose name itself means the LORD saves.
study.god: God is celebrated here as warrior, utterly incomparable in holiness, and shepherd all at once, powerful enough to destroy an empire's army yet gentle enough to lead His people with loving devotion. His holiness is not cold distance but the very reason He can be trusted completely; no rival god shares His character or His power.

crossReferences.note: The redeemed sing the song of Moses and of the Lamb together after the final victory over evil.
A later psalmist recalls this same sea crossing to renew his own faith in God's saving power.
Echoes this song's language, the LORD is my strength and my song, He has become my salvation.
The Song of Deborah follows the same pattern, victory retold in poetry as an act of worship.

study.oneStory: Fresh out of slavery, Israel's first instinct is to sing, crediting God alone for a victory only He could win, and trusting Him to finish bringing them home. That pattern of redemption answered by praise runs the length of Scripture and arrives at its final scene in Revelation, where the redeemed sing this very song beside the song of the Lamb, the greater and final warrior who has triumphed gloriously.

quiz.q: What does the opening line of the song credit to the LORD?

options.text: That He is highly exalted, having thrown the horse and rider into the sea.
options.note: Verse 1 gives this exact confession; the victory belongs to God alone.

options.text: That Israel's own military skill won the day.
options.note: The song explicitly credits the LORD, not Israel's own strength or strategy.

options.text: That Moses personally defeated Pharaoh's army.
options.note: Moses leads the song, but the praise is directed to the LORD, not to Moses as a warrior.

options.text: That the Egyptians simply retreated on their own.
options.note: The song describes decisive divine destruction of the army, not a voluntary retreat.

quiz.q: How does the song describe the LORD in verse 3?

options.text: As a warrior, whose name is the LORD.
options.note: The Hebrew names Him plainly, ish milchamah, a man of war, a warrior.

options.text: As a distant judge uninvolved in the battle.
options.note: The song's whole point is God's active, personal involvement in the fight.

options.text: As merely an advisor to Moses.
options.note: The song presents God as the one who actually fought and won, not a passive advisor.

options.text: The song gives no description of God's character.
options.note: Verse 3 explicitly describes Him as a warrior.

quiz.q: What unanswered question forms the heart of the song's praise in verse 11?

options.text: Who among the gods is like You, O LORD, majestic in holiness?
options.note: This rhetorical question asserts God's total incomparability among every rival power.

options.text: Who will lead Israel into the promised land?
options.note: That question is answered elsewhere; verse 11's question is about God's unmatched holiness and power.

options.text: Why did Pharaoh pursue Israel?
options.note: The song is not asking why Pharaoh pursued; it is praising God's incomparable character.

options.text: The song contains no rhetorical questions.
options.note: Verse 11 is explicitly framed as a question with an assumed answer, no one.

quiz.q: What does the song anticipate God doing for His people in verses 13 and 17?

options.text: Leading them with loving devotion to His own holy dwelling place.
options.note: These verses look ahead to God bringing Israel to His sanctuary, His holy mountain.

options.text: Abandoning them once they crossed the sea.
options.note: The opposite is promised; God will lead and guide them onward with loving devotion.

options.text: Returning them to slavery in Egypt.
options.note: The song looks forward to the promised land, not a return to bondage.

options.text: No future hope is expressed in the song.
options.note: Verses 13 and 17 explicitly express forward-looking hope and promise.

quiz.q: Who leads the women in an answering refrain at the end of the passage?

options.text: Miriam the prophetess, Aaron's sister, with a tambourine.
options.note: Verse 20 names her directly and describes her leading role in the worship.

options.text: Zipporah, the wife of Moses.
options.note: The text names Miriam, not Zipporah, as the one leading the women's song.

options.text: Aaron himself leads the women in song.
options.note: The text specifically credits Miriam, described as a prophetess, with leading the women.

options.text: No one leads a second song; the passage ends after verse 18.
options.note: Verses 20 to 21 explicitly describe Miriam's separate, answering refrain.

questions: Israel's first act of freedom was worship, not planning or complaining. What is the first thing you tend to do after God clearly answers a hard prayer, and does it look like this song?
The song credits the LORD alone for the victory, refusing to let Israel take any glory for themselves. Where are you tempted to take credit for something that was really God's work in your life?
The unanswered question, 'who is like You, majestic in holiness,' assumes there is no rival. What false gods, however subtle, compete for that place of incomparable trust in your own life?

The song looks forward in hope even while still fresh from crisis, trusting God to finish what He started. What situation in your life needs that same forward-looking trust rather than just relief that today's danger has passed?
Miriam led the women in their own refrain, a whole community joining the same worship together. Who could you bring alongside you this week to praise God together for something He has done?

translationNotes.note: My father's God echoes the covenant language used with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, tying this new act of salvation to God's much older promises to the patriarchs.
The blast of Your nostrils is vivid poetic imagery for God's powerful breath or wind, matching the historical description of the strong east wind in Exodus 14:21.

The mountain of Your inheritance likely refers to the land of Canaan broadly, or Mount Zion specifically as its later center of worship, though this song was composed before either the conquest or the building of the temple.
